Score 80% or higher to generate your certificate of completion. Review Course Certification Home Student Name Course / Class Period Passing score: 80% or higher. Answer every question before submitting. 1. What should be checked before submitting a 3D print? Only the file name. Only whether it looks cool. File orientation, scale, supports, material, and print time. Only the color. 2. Why does print orientation matter? It affects strength, support needs, surface quality, and time. It only changes the thumbnail. It has no effect on strength. It only matters for resin printing. 3. What should you do if a print starts spaghetti, detaches, or fails? Pull the filament while printing. Hide the failed part. Stop or report the failed print according to class procedure. Let it run all day. 4. Why should unsupported overhangs be reviewed? They can sag or fail without support or better orientation. They always print perfectly. They only affect the color. They make the printer faster. 5. What is a reasonable fabrication request? Answer every question before submitting. 1. What is the safest assumption about a soldering iron tip? It cools instantly after use. It only heats the solder. It is hot enough to burn skin or materials. It is safe if it is shiny. 2. What should you do before powering a circuit? Check polarity, wiring, and instructor expectations. Connect power first and troubleshoot later. Use the highest voltage available. Hold wires together by hand. 3. What does a good solder joint usually look like? Loose but covered in solder. Connected to every nearby pad. Smooth, shiny, and secure without bridges to nearby pads. Large, dull, and blob-shaped. 4. Why is fume awareness important? Soldering can produce fumes that should not be breathed directly. Fumes improve solder flow. Fumes show the circuit works. Fumes only matter outdoors. 5. It does not need to be perfect, but it should be clear enough that someone else can understand the design intent. Shape Show the general form of the solution. Function Show how the design works or what each major part does. Annotations Add labels, arrows, notes, and explanations. Differences Each concept should be meaningfully different, not just the same idea redrawn. Sketching Expectations Create at least three different concepts. Label major parts and features. Show how the payload is protected or delivered. Identify materials or manufacturing ideas when possible. Use arrows or notes to explain motion, deployment, or recovery. Open Unit 1 Media Hub Criteria vs. Constraints Engineering problems are shaped by what the design must do and what limits the design must follow. Criteria Criteria describe what makes a design successful. Deliver payload safely Be lightweight Be reliable Be easy to recover Protect fragile components Constraints Constraints are limits or restrictions on the design. Limited materials Maximum size or mass Time limit Cost limit Safety requirements Aerospace Trade-Offs Mass vs. Strength A stronger part may be heavier. A lighter part may fail more easily. Cost vs. Performance The best-performing design may not be realistic within the available budget. Complexity vs. Reliability More moving parts may add function but increase the chance of failure. Engineers often return to earlier steps as they learn more. 1 Define the Problem Clarify the mission, user need, criteria, constraints, and stakeholders. 2 Research Study existing systems, materials, failures, and design approaches. 3 Imagine Generate multiple possible ideas without choosing too early. 4 Plan Select a promising idea using evidence, trade-offs, and decision tools. 5 Create Build a model, prototype, sketch package, or simulation. 6 Test & Improve Collect data, identify weaknesses, revise, and communicate next steps. Open Unit 1 Media Hub Why Engineers Research Research helps engineers understand what has already been tried, what works, what fails, and what design features may be useful. Research should inspire your thinking, not replace it. Look For Functions How does the system move, protect, deploy, land, or recover? Look For Materials What materials are used and why might they have been selected? Look For Failures What can go wrong and how do engineers reduce that risk? Look For Trade-Offs What did the designers prioritize, and what did they sacrifice? Research Notes Format Source / Example What It Does Useful Design Feature What We Should Avoid Copying Directly Key Rule Research should help you make better design decisions. Your final concept must be your own original combination of ideas, constraints, and justifications.

Open Unit 3 Media Hub Details Matter Small CAD features can affect strength, fit, weight, manufacturing, and safety. Aerospace parts often need careful edge treatments, mounting features, and lightweight geometry. Feature Uses Holes: fasteners, alignment, mounting Fillets: reduce sharp edges and stress concentration Chamfers: improve edges and assembly fit Shell: reduce weight in housings Patterns: repeat ribs or holes Design Trade-Off Removing material can reduce weight, but it may also reduce strength. Aerospace designers constantly balance weight, strength, cost, and manufacturability. Student Task Revise a basic component by adding holes, fillets/chamfers, and at least one lightweighting feature. Explain why each feature was added.

Open Unit 3 Media Hub What Is Parametric Modeling? Parametric modeling means a CAD model is controlled by dimensions, constraints, and relationships. When a value changes, the model updates in a predictable way. Why It Matters Parts can be revised quickly Design intent is preserved Dimensions can be adjusted after testing Teams can make controlled changes Manufacturing versions can be created Aerospace Example A payload bracket may need different hole spacing, wall thickness, or overall width after testing. A parametric model lets engineers update those features without rebuilding the entire part. Student Task Create a simple parametric part, change at least three dimensions, and describe how the model updated.

keywords: Unit 4: Tolerances and Fit | Lockwood STEM Skip to main content Introduction to Engineering Design Unit 4: Tolerances & Fit Understand acceptable variation, clearance, interference, and why aerospace parts need precision. Open Unit 4 Media Hub Tolerance Means Acceptable Variation No manufactured part is perfect. Tolerance tells the maker and inspector how much variation is acceptable while the part still functions correctly. Key Terms Nominal dimension: target size Tolerance: allowed variation Clearance fit: parts have space between them Interference fit: parts are intentionally tight Critical dimension: size that strongly affects function Aerospace Examples Fastener holes must align Payload mounts must fit securely Panels must not interfere with moving parts Printed parts may need clearance for assembly Laser-cut slots must match material thickness Student Task Given several dimensions and tolerance ranges, decide whether each part is acceptable, too large, or too small. Then identify which dimensions are critical for fit.
